text.title: GraphQL Security and Antipatterns
text.summary: Depth limits, complexity caps, persisted queries, per-resolver auth, breaking change detection, and common failure modes.

rule.statement: Enforce a query depth limit (5-8 levels) and complexity cap (e.g., 1000 units) before any resolver runs. Both limits must be middleware, not per-resolver checks. An absence of either allows a single malicious query to DoS the server.
rule.rationale: Without depth limits, a query can request arbitrarily deep nested relationships, causing N^depth DB queries. Complexity caps prevent unbounded list traversals like querying all users' all orders' all items.

rule.statement: Implement authorization per-resolver or via a @auth directive on schema fields. Never rely solely on gateway-level auth — a resolver reachable from an unguarded query path leaks data even when the gateway blocks direct access.
rule.rationale: GraphQL queries can reach any field from multiple paths. A field accessible via an unrelated query traversal bypasses per-route gateway auth entirely.

rule.statement: Run graphql-inspector diff against the production schema on every PR. Fail the build on any breaking change that lacks an explicit migration note. Use @deprecated(reason: "use X instead") for field removal, removing only after analytics confirm zero usage.
rule.rationale: GraphQL has no built-in versioning. A field removal or type change silently breaks all clients using that field. graphql-inspector makes breaking changes an explicit, reviewable decision.

rule.statement: Enable persisted queries in production: clients send a hash, the server resolves the SDL. This eliminates arbitrary query injection DoS and slashes payload bandwidth by 60-90%.
rule.rationale: Ad-hoc query execution allows any authenticated user to submit maximally expensive queries. Persisted queries lock the server to known, pre-analyzed query shapes.

antipattern.reason: Caches results from one request and serves them to subsequent requests, leaking data between users. Per-request instantiation is mandatory.
subscriptions-transport-ws is unmaintained and has known issues. The current standard is graphql-ws. Many server libraries still default to the old protocol — opt in explicitly.

antipattern.description: Input types with nested optional objects creating impossible validation shapes.
antipattern.reason: Deeply nested optional inputs produce 2^n possible shapes that are impossible to validate exhaustively. Use flat, single-purpose input types: CreateOrderInput, UpdateOrderStatusInput.

antipattern.description: Adding a new resolver field without an accompanying DataLoader for 1:N traversal.
antipattern.reason: Every parent object triggers a separate DB query. 100 user nodes in a list query = 100 separate order queries. Always require an XByYLoader for any 1:N field.

antipattern.description: Mixing schema-first and code-first approaches in the same service without documentation.
antipattern.reason: Codegen output drift between the two approaches causes type mismatches that surface only at runtime. Pick one approach and document it in CLAUDE.md.
